4 destination/article regions and 7 visible headings detected.
8 monetary values detected; budget wording present.
button "Vote for Copenhagen" changed visible text or control state.
Winner or itinerary content was visible or reachable. 0 visible itinerary regions detected.
1 enabled controls lacked a detectable name; 11 unique controls received focus across 12 Tab presses.

guardrailFacts.corePathDefinition: npm run check failed, or at least two core acceptance items failed at both viewports.

findings.title: The displayed winner is controlled by card selection rather than vote totals
findings.evidence: `selected` and `voted` are independent state. `trip` is derived only from `selected`, while `toggleVote` changes only `voted`; no winner is derived from the vote counts. Consequently, clicking Porto or Annecy can make that destination appear as the leading/current favorite and drive its itinerary even while Copenhagen retains the highest count.
findings.impact: Voting does not determine the winning destination, so the core group decision and winner itinerary can contradict the visible results.
findings.remediation: Derive the winning trip from current vote totals. If selection is needed for comparison, keep it separate and do not label it as the leader or use it as the winner itinerary.

findings.title: Destination details mix dynamic totals with Copenhagen-specific values
findings.evidence: The selected trip's travel time and total budget are dynamic, but the transit icon/origin and budget breakdown are hard-coded. Selecting Porto shows a €412 total while the displayed €146 travel, €178 stay, and €141 food values sum to €465; Annecy similarly shows €388 against the same €465 breakdown. Both also retain a train/Hbf presentation despite their plane/car modes.
findings.impact: Users comparing destinations receive internally inconsistent cost and transport information for two of the three choices, undermining a core planning task.
findings.remediation: Store transport details and a summing budget breakdown per trip, then render those fields from the selected destination.

findings.title: Enabled Explore and Chat navigation controls are inert
findings.evidence: The enabled `Explore` and `Chat` buttons have no click handler, navigation target, disabled state, or unavailable-state explanation, while adjacent Overview and Itinerary controls do change views.
findings.impact: These secondary navigation actions promise reachable product areas but provide no response when activated.
findings.remediation: Implement the corresponding view/navigation, or present the features as unavailable rather than enabled buttons.

findings.title: Itinerary actions confirm changes that are never applied
findings.evidence: The itinerary is recreated from a fixed `days` array. `Add activity` only calls `notify("An activity slot was added")`, and `Start booking` only reports that a checklist was created; neither handler updates state or reveals the claimed result.
findings.impact: Users receive false success feedback for planning actions, although the itinerary and booking state remain unchanged.
findings.remediation: Persist the resulting activity/checklist in component state and display it, or make these controls clearly unavailable in the sample.

findings.title: Tabs and vote toggles expose their state only visually
findings.evidence: Active tabs are represented only by an `active` CSS class without `aria-selected` or equivalent state. Vote buttons always use `aria-label="Vote for …"` and communicate selection only through a class and heart fill, without `aria-pressed`; after voting, the accessible action name also incorrectly continues to promise adding a vote.
findings.impact: Screen-reader users can operate these core controls but cannot reliably determine the selected view or whether their vote is currently applied.
findings.remediation: Use tablist/tab semantics with `aria-selected`, and expose vote buttons as toggles with `aria-pressed` and an action name consistent with the current state.

findings.title: The budget disclosure control has no accessible name
findings.evidence: The enabled `round-icon` button contains only a `ChevronDown` icon and has no text or ARIA label. Deterministic desktop and mobile checks each found exactly one enabled unnamed control, matching this button on the initial view.
findings.impact: Assistive-technology users encounter an unnamed button and cannot identify its budget-related purpose.
findings.remediation: Add a concise accessible name describing the action, and expose expanded state if it is intended as a disclosure.

findings.title: Small comparison and itinerary metadata has insufficient contrast
findings.evidence: Normal-size text includes `#9b959c` at 9–10px on white (approximately 2.93:1), `#9a949b` at 11px on white (approximately 2.96:1), and `#8f8990` at 11px on white (approximately 3.42:1). These selectors carry estimate notes and itinerary dates/times, below the 4.5:1 contrast expected for normal text.
findings.impact: Low-vision users may struggle to read cost context and itinerary timing information.
findings.remediation: Darken these metadata colors to at least 4.5:1 against their actual backgrounds, or use qualifying large-text treatment where appropriate.

categories.label: Brief fidelity and functional correctness
evidence: `source/src/App.tsx:21-25` defines three destinations with visible travel and per-person budget data; deterministic checks passed destination, budget, and travel-time detection at both viewports.
The deterministic vote check passed at both viewports, and `source/src/App.tsx:45-47` implements a visible vote toggle.

The winner/itinerary check reports a pass but zero visible itinerary regions. Source confirms a direct path: `source/src/App.tsx:81-85` switches to the itinerary and renders it, while the findings document incorrect winner derivation and destination-specific details.
Several secondary controls remain inert or acknowledge state changes that do not occur.

categories.label: Reliability and state integrity
evidence: `source/src/App.tsx:43-47` uses a functional vote-state update and derives the displayed total from the current vote array, avoiding simple count drift during ordinary toggles.
Deterministic desktop and mobile paths recorded zero console errors and zero page errors.
Winner identity is not derived from vote state, producing the documented state contradiction between counts, leading labels, and itinerary.

categories.label: Accessibility
evidence: `source/src/index.css:4` supplies a visible `:focus-visible` outline for buttons, and deterministic checks focused 11 unique controls during each 12-Tab path.
Both deterministic viewport checks failed accessible controls because one enabled control was unnamed; source confirms the unlabeled icon-only budget button.
Core toggle/tab state lacks programmatic semantics, and several small metadata colors remain below normal-text contrast requirements.

categories.label: Maintainability and code clarity
evidence: `source/src/App.tsx:8-35` centralizes destination data in a typed `Trip` model and isolates travel-icon selection; `source/src/App.tsx:92-98` separates the itinerary view from the main component.
TypeScript strict mode is enabled in `source/tsconfig.json:2-17`, and the supplied typecheck completed successfully.
No separate material maintainability defect was found beyond the functional hard-coding already reported under correctness.

categories.label: Security, privacy, and dependency compliance
evidence: The supplied dependency comparison passed with no added, removed, or changed packages.
Deterministic evidence found no remote references or external browser requests, and the source uses no unsafe HTML injection or user-data collection.
No actionable security, privacy, or dependency-compliance issue was found.

categories.label: Performance and responsive robustness
evidence: The production assets total 271,152 bytes; the supplied build reports 55.45 kB CSS and 215.23 kB JavaScript before gzip.
Deterministic desktop and mobile checks each measured 0px document-level horizontal overflow.

`source/src/index.css:15` collapses destination, details, and itinerary grids to one column on narrow screens, and there are no remote media dependencies. No material performance or responsive defect was found.

categories.label: Verification readiness
evidence: The supplied `npm run check` passed with exit code 0, including `tsc --noEmit` and a Vite production build.
`source/package.json:6-10` provides explicit typecheck, build, and combined check scripts, while `source/tsconfig.json:10-17` enables strict checking and no-emit validation.
The deterministic hashes before and after checks match, confirming the inspected source was unchanged. No separate verification-readiness defect was found.

strengths: The app supplies three realistic destinations, visible budgets and travel times, an interactive vote toggle, and a directly reachable three-day itinerary without sign-in or backend access.
The supplied build/typecheck succeeds, both tested viewports avoid horizontal overflow, and the defined browser paths produce no uncaught errors.
Dependencies remain within the frozen starter, with no remote assets, external requests, or unsafe rendering mechanism.
Responsive grid rules and visible button focus styling provide a solid baseline for desktop, mobile, and keyboard use.
risks: The group can be shown a manually selected destination as the winner even when its vote count is lower.
Porto and Annecy display misleading budget composition and transport context in the detail panel.
Screen-reader users cannot reliably perceive vote/tab state, and low-contrast metadata creates an additional low-vision barrier.
Several enabled secondary actions either do nothing or report successful changes that are not represented in state.
recommendation: Fix winner derivation and destination-specific cost/transport data first. Then expose control state and names programmatically, improve metadata contrast, and either implement or clearly disable the placeholder navigation and itinerary actions.
